original_abstract:
This study details the synthesis, structural characterization, and theoretical exploration of a novel coumarin-triazole-thiophene hybrid: 4-(((4-ethyl-5-(thiophen-2-yl)-4H-1,2,4-triazol-3-yl)thio)methyl)-6,7-dimethyl-2H-chromen-2-one (1). The compound was synthesized via the reaction of 4-ethyl-5-(thiophen-2-yl)-4H-1,2,4-triazole-3-thiol and 4-(chloromethyl)-6,7-dimethyl-2H-chromen-2-one and subsequently characterized using microanalysis, IR, 1H, and 13C APT NMR spectroscopy. Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations were employed to analyze the gas-phase molecular geometry and electronic properties, while reactivity descriptors and molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) mapping pinpointed the molecule’s reactive centers.

In silico ADMET profiling indicated that compound 1 possesses favorable gastrointestinal absorption but lacks human blood-brain barrier permeability; according to toxicity model reports, the hybrid falls into toxicity class 4. To evaluate its potential therapeutic utility, molecular docking simulations were performed against a range of SARS-CoV-2 proteins. The results demonstrate that the title compound effectively interacts with all tested proteins, showing a particularly high affinity for the Papain-like protease (PLpro). These binding interactions and the overall stability of the protein-ligand complexes were further validated through molecular dynamics simulations.
humanized_abstract:
In this study, a new coumarin-triazole-thiophene hybrid compound named 4-(((4-ethyl-5-(thiophen-2-yl)-4H-1,2,4-triazol-3-yl)thio)methyl)-6,7-dimethyl-2H-chromen-2-one (1) was produced by the reaction of 4-ethyl-5-(thiophen-2-yl)-4H-1,2,4-triazole-3-thiol and 4-(chloromethyl)-6,7-dimethyl-2H-chromen-2-one and structural features were explained by microanalysis, IR, 1H and 13C APT NMR spectroscopic data. Gas-phase molecular structure and electronic properties of compound 1 were investigated by means of Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations and reactivity descriptors and molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) mapping was performed in order to explain reactive sites of the compound.

Compound 1 had good GI absorption and did not cross the blood-brain barrier in humans according to the in silico ADMET profiling. The toxicity prediction model shows that the hybrid falls into toxicity class 4. To explore the potential drug-like activity for the synthesized hybrid, the compound 1 was subjected to molecular docking studies against various SARS-CoV-2 proteins. And the hybrid exhibited strong binding interactions with the studied SARS-CoV-2 proteins as predicted by MolDock 3.0, and a very strong interaction with Papain-like protease (PLpro). The protein-ligand binding models as well as the stability of complexes were evaluated and confirmed through molecular dynamics (MD) simulations.

document.output:
In this study, a new coumarin-triazole-thiophene hybrid compound named 4-(((4-ethyl-5-(thiophen-2-yl)-4H-1,2,4-triazol-3-yl)thio)methyl)-6,7-dimethyl-2H-chromen-2-one (1) was produced by the reaction of 4-ethyl-5-(thiophen-2-yl)-4H-1,2,4-triazole-3-thiol and 4-(chloromethyl)-6,7-dimethyl-2H-chromen-2-one and structural features were explained by microanalysis, IR, 1H and 13C APT NMR spectroscopic data. Gas-phase molecular structure and electronic properties of compound 1 were investigated by means of Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations and reactivity descriptors and molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) mapping was performed in order to explain reactive sites of the compound.

Compound 1 had good GI absorption and did not cross the blood-brain barrier in humans according to the in silico ADMET profiling. The toxicity prediction model shows that the hybrid falls into toxicity class 4. To explore the potential drug-like activity for the synthesized hybrid, the compound 1 was subjected to molecular docking studies against various SARS-CoV-2 proteins. And the hybrid exhibited strong binding interactions with the studied SARS-CoV-2 proteins as predicted by MolDock 3.0, and a very strong interaction with Papain-like protease (PLpro). The protein-ligand binding models as well as the stability of complexes were evaluated and confirmed through molecular dynamics (MD) simulations.
document.input:
This study details the synthesis, structural characterization, and theoretical exploration of a novel coumarin-triazole-thiophene hybrid: 4-(((4-ethyl-5-(thiophen-2-yl)-4H-1,2,4-triazol-3-yl)thio)methyl)-6,7-dimethyl-2H-chromen-2-one (1). The compound was synthesized via the reaction of 4-ethyl-5-(thiophen-2-yl)-4H-1,2,4-triazole-3-thiol and 4-(chloromethyl)-6,7-dimethyl-2H-chromen-2-one and subsequently characterized using microanalysis, IR, 1H, and 13C APT NMR spectroscopy. Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations were employed to analyze the gas-phase molecular geometry and electronic properties, while reactivity descriptors and molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) mapping pinpointed the molecule’s reactive centers.

In silico ADMET profiling indicated that compound 1 possesses favorable gastrointestinal absorption but lacks human blood-brain barrier permeability; according to toxicity model reports, the hybrid falls into toxicity class 4. To evaluate its potential therapeutic utility, molecular docking simulations were performed against a range of SARS-CoV-2 proteins. The results demonstrate that the title compound effectively interacts with all tested proteins, showing a particularly high affinity for the Papain-like protease (PLpro). These binding interactions and the overall stability of the protein-ligand complexes were further validated through molecular dynamics simulations.
